newHtml:
Captives: sex permitted, withdrawal irrelevant, resale preserved Warfare & Jihad Sexual Issues Slavery & Captives Strong Nasa'i #3362
"We took captives and used azl. The Prophet said: 'It does not matter whether you do or not — no soul decreed to exist will fail to exist.'"

What the hadith says

Muhammad's ruling on coitus interruptus during sex with captives: indifferent to the practice. The underlying act — sex with captives — is unquestioned; only the contraceptive method is at issue.

Why this is a problem

  1. The rape is assumed; the fatwa is about birth control. The captives' consent is never raised, and the soldiers' concern — pregnancy reduces resale value — drives the question.
  2. Commercial logic governs the discussion, not ethics. Muhammad's answer addresses predestination theology, not the morality of the act.

Why it fails

A theology whose entire engagement with "sex with captured women" consists of a fatwa about withdrawal timing has accepted the underlying transaction and moved to adjust its parameters. The "Islam regulated but didn't invent" framing still leaves the canonical record of prophetic engagement with captive-sex economics as the operational precedent ISIS cited in 2014 with explicit classical footnoting.

Ma'iz fled mid-stoning; the crowd pursued him to the rocks Hudud Prophetic Character Strong Nasa'i #7143
"When the stones struck Ma'iz, he fled. They chased him to al-Harrah and stoned him to death there. The Prophet said: 'Why did you not let him go?'"

What the hadith says

Nasa'i preserves the Ma'iz stoning — including his mid-execution flight, the crowd's pursuit, and Muhammad's post-mortem question about why they hadn't stopped.

Why this is a problem

  1. His flight proves non-consent to his own execution. The confessor who ran was demonstrably not a willing participant in his own purification, undermining the "he sought expiation" framing.
  2. The Prophet's regret arrived after the stones did their work. "Why didn't you let him go?" was spoken over a corpse, not before the killing.

Why it fails

The "procedural retraction" interpretation is a post-hoc juristic refinement. In the canonical event the crowd did not stop — they chased him down and killed him — and the Prophet's response came after the fact. A mercy whose expression is post-mortem is not procedural protection; it is retrospective theatre. The hadith documents execution proceeding despite flight, which is the operational precedent, whatever later commentary tried to make of it.

Al-Ghamidiyya — breastfed two years, then stoned while her child watched Hudud Women Strong Nasa'i #5406
"The Prophet deferred her until she gave birth, then until she weaned the child; then he ordered her stoned."

What the hadith says

A pregnant confessing woman's execution was deferred through childbirth and a two-year nursing period, then carried out. The woman's fate was sealed from the moment Muhammad accepted her confession.

Why this is a problem

  1. Two years of care followed by execution proves the system recognised her motherhood — and killed her anyway. Pastoral concern during the deferral made the execution more premeditated, not less.
  2. The child is left a motherless weaned toddler by the state. The community's formal procedure produced this outcome without treating it as a problem.

Why it fails

Methodical patience before execution is not clemency — it is premeditation. The moral profile of a weaned toddler orphaned by formal state procedure is not improved by the care taken along the way. Muhammad's post-death praise — "she has repented in a manner that would suffice seventy of the people of Medina" — is precisely the frame that makes execution coherent within the system: death for sexual transgression is spiritually beneficial for the executed. That framing is the problem, not its resolution.

Aisha married at six, consummated at nine — Nasa'i's version Child Marriage Prophetic Character Strong Nasa'i #3378
"The Prophet married me when I was six years old; the marriage was consummated when I was nine."

What the hadith says

Nasa'i preserves the same testimony as Bukhari, Muslim, and Ibn Majah — Aisha's own words about her marriage age and consummation age.

Why this is a problem

  1. Five canonical collections make the "revisionist late-dating" impossible without rejecting the hadith corpus wholesale. The age-9 testimony is Aisha's own, cross-attested across all major collections.
  2. The floor of the tradition is a nine-year-old. Classical fiqh's minimum-age jurisprudence derived directly from this hadith, and it remains operative in jurisdictions that permit child marriage today.

Why it fails

The revisionist-age argument requires rejecting Aisha's own testimony, the consensus of all five canonical collections, and the overwhelming classical-scholar consensus — in favour of alternative historical speculation with no canonical grounding. Rejecting the hadith's age figure is a reform position, not a retrieval of the tradition's actual teaching.

A virgin's silence is her consent to marriage Child Marriage Women Governance Strong Nasa'i #3266
"A virgin is consulted about her marriage — her silence is her consent."

What the hadith says

A father can marry off a virgin daughter; her silence is legally sufficient consent. For a prepubescent girl, no consent process is required at all under classical jabr doctrine.

Why this is a problem

  1. Coercive consent architecture — silence equals agreement under family pressure. A frightened or uncomprehending girl has no structural means to object.
  2. The classical jabr doctrine extended this to prepubescent cases where no consultation was required at all, showing the rule operated where active objection was already impossible.

Why it fails

The "protects her consent" framing inverts the actual legal mechanism: the rule defaults to agreement, placing the burden on the silent girl to actively object against family pressure. A consent architecture that counts silence as agreement has written the exit condition out of the contract. The jabr doctrine — marrying off daughters before puberty without any consent process — shows the rule functioned precisely where objection was psychologically blocked.

Zaynab — adopted son's wife, then Muhammad's own Prophetic Character Women Contradictions Strong Q 33:37
Nasa'i preserves Q 33:37 commentary: Zayd (Muhammad's adopted son) divorced Zaynab; Muhammad married her; a verse abolished adoption to enable the marriage.

What the hadith says

The marriage to Zaynab required abolishing adoption as a legal category in Islamic law — a universal rule change generated by one personal scenario.

Why this is a problem

  1. A universal rule (no adoption) was generated from one private marriage. Muslim orphans for 1,400 years have been denied full legal adoption as a direct consequence.
  2. Q 33:37 itself acknowledges the marriage caused discomfort — framing the Prophet's convenience as universal theological legislation.

Why it fails

The "correcting a false taboo" framing requires accepting that correcting one pre-Islamic custom required permanently denying millions of orphaned children legal family status. Islamic law permits guardianship but not full legal adoption with inheritance rights — a restriction actively harmful to orphans and derived entirely from one Prophetic marriage. Q 33:37's own text records the social discomfort contemporaries noticed in the timing.

Wife leaving home without permission — angels curse her Women Moral Problems Strong Ibn Majah #5325
Nasa'i preserves the tradition: "If she leaves her home without permission, the angels curse her until she returns."

What the hadith says

A wife's freedom of movement requires male permission — absence of which triggers angelic cursing until she returns.

Why this is a problem

  1. The home becomes a legally enforced zone of confinement. Permission to leave is the husband's unilateral prerogative with no specified conditions.
  2. Divine enforcement turns movement restriction into a cosmological fact rather than a cultural custom, removing it from the domain of ordinary reform.

Why it fails

The exceptions are juristically elaborated but the plain text carries no such qualifications. Saudi Arabia's guardianship system, which until recently required male permission for women to travel, work, or access healthcare, operated on precisely this canonical structure. A religion whose heavens curse a woman for walking out her front door has built confinement into its doorframe, whatever later exceptions soften the edges.

Safiyyah consummation — the night her family was killed Prophetic Character Sexual Issues Strong Nasa'i #3380
"The Prophet took Safiyyah at Khaybar after the killing of her husband and father."

What the hadith says

Safiyyah was captured after Muhammad's forces killed her father and husband at Khaybar. Muhammad took her as wife/concubine and consummation occurred on the same journey, with the istibra waiting period reportedly waived.

Why this is a problem

  1. Her father was killed days before; her husband Kinana had been tortured and executed. Her people were being enslaved around her. The hadith addresses none of this.
  2. The waiting period was waived — same-journey consummation while her mourning was acute and her community's destruction was ongoing.

Why it fails

Her eventual status does not rewrite the circumstances of the wedding night, which is what the hadith preserves. Safiyyah's father was killed at Khaybar; her husband was tortured and killed; her community was being enslaved. "Royal marriage" as a framing does not recover ordinary consent from that timeline. A prophet whose wedding night followed the killing of his wife's father and husband has defined love on terms a modern ethical framework cannot rehabilitate.

Jizya with humiliation — the tax of subjugation Disbelievers Governance Strong Q 9:29
"Until they give jizya with willing submission and feel themselves subdued."

What the hadith says

Nasa'i's classical commentary insists the humiliation aspect is essential to the jizya — payment without subjugation defeats its theological purpose.

Why this is a problem

  1. Religious taxation paired explicitly with ritualised humiliation. The payment is not merely financial — it is performative subjugation of non-Muslim subjects.
  2. The Quranic text (Q 9:29) itself encodes "feeling subdued" as the goal — this is not a commentator's addition but the scriptural frame.

Why it fails

The classical commentators were explicit: the humiliation was not incidental but essential. Ibn Qayyim and al-Mawardi described payment protocols intended to make the dhimmi's inferior status physically visible in the transaction. A tax designed to make the taxpayer "feel subdued" has never been primarily about revenue — it communicates whose faith is second-class. The modern "protection-tax" framing is a euphemism the classical tradition did not use.

A Muslim is not killed for a disbeliever — Nasa'i's preservation Disbelievers Hudud Strong Nasa'i #4738
"A Muslim is not to be killed for a disbeliever."

What the hadith says

Asymmetry in blood law: a Muslim who kills a non-Muslim does not face qisas (equal retaliation). Non-Muslim lives are legally worth less before the court.

Why this is a problem

  1. Structural inequality in justice by religion. The court system explicitly assigns different legal value to believer and non-believer lives.
  2. Still enforced in multiple modern jurisdictions. Saudi Arabian and Pakistani courts operate under different blood-money valuations for Muslim and non-Muslim victims.

Why it fails

The "different covenant status" framing makes the inequality a principled design feature, not an accident. A court that does not execute a Muslim for killing a non-Muslim has declared whose life it protects and whose it discounts. The structural message of the law — that a Muslim life has higher value before the court than a non-Muslim life — is not neutralised by framing it as covenant-status differentiation.

"May Allah curse the Jews and Christians" — Muhammad's deathbed Antisemitism Prophetic Character Strong Nasa'i #703
"May Allah curse the Jews and Christians, for they took the graves of their prophets as places of worship."

What the hadith says

On his deathbed, Muhammad cursed Jews and Christians by name. The deathbed context gives the utterance the weight of final testament.

Why this is a problem

  1. Deathbed weight makes this final testament, not casual remark. Classical scholars treated a dying man's last priorities as especially significant.
  2. The curse is collective, not behavioural. "Jews and Christians" names communities, not only individuals who venerate graves.

Why it fails

"Curse" in Islamic theological vocabulary carries specific weight beyond a warning. Classical commentators treated the deathbed utterance as a statement about the communities' spiritual status, not merely a procedural rebuke. The selective application is telling: the rule is invoked against Jewish and Christian grave-veneration but not against Muslim pilgrimage to Muhammad's tomb in Medina, where millions annually visit a grave. A rule applied outward but not inward is polemical, not principled.

70,000 Jews of Isfahan will follow the Dajjal Antisemitism Eschatology Strong Muslim #2944
"The Dajjal will be followed by seventy thousand Jews of Isfahan, wearing Persian shawls."

What the hadith says

The end-times Antichrist figure will have a specifically Jewish army, identified by ethnicity and geography.

Why this is a problem

  1. An entire ethno-religious group is scripted as the Antichrist's foot soldiers. This is a prophetic assignment of a people to the role of ultimate evil's army.
  2. Cited in modern antisemitic Muslim rhetoric as scriptural justification for anti-Jewish enmity, with canonical status making it unavailable for dismissal.

Why it fails

The "eschatological only" framing does not neutralise the text's operational use. Contemporary antisemitic Muslim rhetoric cites this hadith as a statement about Jewish eschatological alignment with evil. A scripture-status tradition that assigns an entire ethno-religious community to the role of Antichrist's army establishes the moral category now — the prophecy operates as pre-justification for enmity regardless of when its fulfillment is imagined to occur.
